questions.question: Why does self-focused attention during social situations increase anxiety rather than help manage it?

options.text: Because focusing on yourself uses up too much mental energy
Because self-monitoring increases awareness of anxiety symptoms, prevents you from noticing positive feedback, and interferes with natural conversation flow
Because self-awareness is always harmful in social contexts
Because it causes others to become uncomfortable around you

questions.explanation: Self-focused attention creates a cascade of problems: it increases awareness of anxiety symptoms (the more you monitor, the more you notice), prevents you from seeing positive feedback (you miss friendly smiles and nods), interferes with natural conversation flow (you cannot fully listen while self-monitoring), and makes interactions feel more effortful.

questions.question: What is the 'curiosity approach' to shifting attention outward?

options.text: Researching the other person on social media before meeting them
Asking yourself intrusive questions about why you are anxious
Adopting genuine curiosity about the other person — wondering what you can learn about them, what matters to them, and what is interesting about what they are saying
Being curious about your own internal emotional states during conversation

questions.explanation: The curiosity approach means holding genuine interest-based questions in mind: 'What can I learn about this person?' 'What matters to them?' This works because it gives attention a specific external target, replaces self-judgment with genuine interest, makes conversations more engaging for both people, and generates natural follow-up questions without rehearsal.

questions.question: Why is it recommended to practice external attention in low-stress situations (walking, eating, waiting) before applying it socially?

options.text: Because external attention only works in non-social contexts
Because low-stress practice builds the 'muscle of outward attention' so it is available when you need it in anxiety-provoking social situations
Because you cannot redirect attention during social interactions
Because low-stress environments are the only places where attention matters

questions.explanation: Practicing external attention in low-stress situations — noticing details while walking, focusing on flavors while eating, observing people while waiting — builds the habit before you need it under pressure. Like any skill, it is easier to develop in calm conditions so it becomes automatic and available during more challenging social interactions.

questions.question: How does the 'task-focus technique' help during social situations?

options.text: It helps you complete work tasks while socializing
It gives your attention a concrete external target, occupying the mental space that would otherwise be filled with self-monitoring
It distracts you from the conversation so anxiety cannot develop
It ensures you always have something interesting to say

questions.explanation: The task-focus technique involves giving yourself a specific external task during social situations — like 'learn one new thing about each person I talk to' or 'understand the speaker's main point.' This occupies the mental space that would otherwise be filled with 'Am I being awkward?' and keeps attention directed outward rather than inward.

questions.question: According to the lesson, how quickly can improvements from external attention training appear?

options.text: Only after several months of daily practice
Improvements are not possible without professional guidance
Research shows improvements can appear within as little as one week of consistent practice
Only after completing the entire course

questions.explanation: Research shows that improvements from external attention training can appear within as little as one week of consistent practice. With regular practice, outward attention becomes more natural, self-monitoring decreases, you notice positive feedback you previously missed, and social situations feel less exhausting.
